title: DESIGN.md — Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis (R package)
text: scoring scheme scores linear map standardized observed variables, S = Z W, -level score correlations exactly: scores need materialized — R weight matrices. Waller (2007) derived orthogonal components, identity holds linear W, includes regression (Thurstone, W = R^{-1} Λ), Bartlett, tenBerge scores. standard PCA EFA/ESEM (regression-scored) paths algebra-eligible. Standardization trap. Component/factor scores unit-variance general (Bartlett oblique scores especially). Always divide real score SDs sqrt(diag(W'RW)). Assuming unit variance silently corrupts correlations.
code: cor(S_a, S_b) = D_a^{-1/2} (W_a' R W_b) D_b^{-1/2}            where  R   = input correlation matrix (pearson or polychoric)                   D_x = diag(W_x' R W_x)   # score variances; NOT assumed 1

title: DESIGN.md — Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis (R package)
text: Two situations force scores route: 1. Nonlinear scoring — EAP/MAP/ML scores categorical/ordinal ESEM. (ordinal ESEM instead uses regression scores polychoric loadings, stays linear → algebra polychoric R.) 2. User wants empirical, sample-realized correlations (including FIML/missing-data realities) rather model-implied quantity. missingness differ; document one “edge” represents (default: model-consistent / algebra eligible).
code: compute_edges(levels, R,               method = c("auto", "algebra", "scores"),               pairs  = c("adjacent", "all"),   # "all" for Forbes extension               data   = NULL,               align  = TRUE,               use    = "pairwise") {    for (each pair (a, b) in chosen pairs) {      algebra_ok <- levels[[a]]$scoring$linear &&                   levels[[b]]$scoring$linear && !is.null(R)      if (method == "algebra" || (method == "auto" && algebra_ok)) {       Wa <- levels[[a]]$scoring$weights       Wb <- levels[[b]]$scoring$weights       C  <- crossprod(Wa, R %*% Wb)                       # W_a' R W_b       sa <- sqrt(diag(crossprod(Wa, R %*% Wa)))           # score SDs       sb <- sqrt(diag(crossprod(Wb, R %*% Wb)))       E  <- sweep(sweep(C, 1, sa, "/"), 2, sb, "/")       # standardize     } else {       stopifnot(!is.null(data))                           # nonlinear/EAP or method="scores"       Sa <- score(levels[[a]], data); Sb <- score(levels[[b]], data)       E  <- cor(Sa, Sb, use = use)     }      if (align) E <- align_signs(E, lineage)               # see §7     edges[[pair]] <- E   }   # return: list of (k_a x k_b) matrices  +  tidy edge tibble (from, to, r, is_primary, above_cut) }

title: DESIGN.md — Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis (R package)
text: IDs: models numbered factor count (m1…mK); factors within model m{k}f{j}. Deterministic within-level order = primary-parent (recursive), variance tiebreak. Sort factors within level (primary parent’s position level , descending variance). single top factor ties fall back variance. places child parent splits read cleanly; f{j} ID order follows layout order tables plots agree. Limitation: ordering reduces can’t eliminate crossings (child strong secondary parent elsewhere still throws crossing edge); layout’s barycenter step (§11) refines x-position continuously beyond discrete order. Lineage lives edges/lineage, never ID. child can multiple strong parents (point method), don’t encode parentage m{k}f{j} — breaks structure isn’t clean tree. Matching: assign factor primary parent via greedy per-column argmax |r| (.e. apply(abs(E), 2, .max)). Multiple children sharing parent normal expected — adjacent levels always n_b = n_a + 1 (level k k factors vs k−1 ), pigeonhole least two children share parent. bijection (LSAP/Hungarian) therefore ill-posed removed. Tucker’s φ serves supplementary congruence criterion redundancy_phi filtering (Forbes extension, §14.19) used matching . Sign alignment — anchor primary parent, “positive.” Sign one DoF per factor; make every cross-level correlation positive. Rule: anchor m1f1 defined orientation (positive sum loadings, substantive marker item), orient factor correlation primary parent positive, propagating top-. Document non-primary edges may legitimately negative.

title: DESIGN.md — Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis (R package)
text: Resolved (design round): 1. Rotation → orthogonal CF (cfT, ≈ varimax) across engines, supported rotation; oblique (cfQ) scope (resolved 2026-06 — confounds cross-level signal method’s whole point). Cleaner, communicable cross-level structure; Goldberg-faithful. 2. Correlation basis → pearson default, polychoric opt-, ordinal-detection cli warning (silent switching). 3. Within-level order → primary-parent (recursive), variance tiebreak; f{j} IDs follow . 4. Ordinal ESEM scoring → tenBerge polychoric (linear → algebra) default; EAP opt-. Default Likert path need data edge time. 5. Package name → ackwards (verify via available::available("ackwards")). Resolved build (M1–M3): 6. Ordinal-detection heuristic: ≤ 7 distinct integer values triggers warning. 7. CF kappa: κ = 1/p (varimax-equivalent; Crawford & Ferguson 1970), user-tunable via kappa argument. Note: initial implementation incorrectly used 1/(2p); fixed M4. 8. cor = "spearman" added alongside "pearson" non-polychoric rank-based option. 9. fm argument exposed EFA engine ("minres" default, "ml", "pa"). 10. cut_show argument exposed (default 0.3) control edges flagged above_cut. Resolved M4: 11. ESEM engine → lavaan::efa() (EFA--SEM, full ESEM structural paths). Gives per-level fit indices + rotation-aware SEs + WLSMV ordinal estimation. require full ESEM block syntax; bass-ackwards levels independent EFA solutions. 12. ESEM scoring → self-compute tenBerge weights lavaan’s estimated loadings Λ latent correlation matrix R via existing .tenBerge_weights(R, Λ). lavPredict() used default path (lacks tenBerge); eventual hook EAP opt-. 13. EAP scoring → deferred; EAP request returns cli_abort("yet implemented"). scores-route seam compute_edges() preserved EAP lands. 14. cor = "polychoric" → general basis engines (ESEM-). PCA/EFA compute R via psych::polychoric() ackwards() feed engine usual. ESEM uses lavaan’s latent correlation matrix R edges (mix psych-polychoric R lavaan-WLSMV loadings). 15. ESEM ordinal estimator → WLSMV default; ULSMV documented option. 16. loadings_se → added §4 level contract (p×k matrix, NULL PCA/EFA). Completed M4 (addition items 11–16 ): - NPD polychoric matrices: warn + smooth via psych::cor.smooth() (implemented). - Convergence truncation tested ESEM engine (k=4+ p=6 triggers lavaan error → warn + truncate → object builds k_eff=3). - Algebra-vs-scores cross-check documented Pearson/continuous paths ; polychoric ESEM edges (algebra uses lavaan polychoric R; scores route uses Pearson standardization) diverge design excluded oracle. Resolved M5 (Forbes extension): 17. API → two orthogonal args: pairs = c("adjacent","") prune = c("none","redundant","artefact") (char vector; default "none"). prune != "none" auto-upgrades pairs "" loud cli_inform() (chains need -levels edges assess). 18. Prune action → flag-, never remove. Adds pruned/prune_reason annotation columns edge/node tidy structures; object retains levels (preserves Invariant 5 algebra-vs-scores oracle). Pruning interpretive relabeling, re-estimation — say print/docs (extends §2 honesty caveat). 19. Redundancy → faithful Forbes (2023): score-correlation chains |r| ≥ .9 (default, tunable), retention rule = keep bottom node chain reaches level k (-specific, best-defined), else keep topmost node (broadest manifestation). Tucker’s φ (> .95, Lorenzo-Seva & ten Berge 2006) computed aligned loadings optional conjunctive criterion. φ formula Σaᵢbᵢ / sqrt(Σaᵢ² · Σbᵢ²); base R, new dependency. 20. Additive enrichments paper (default output still matches Forbes’s examples): always report r φ every redundancy candidate (report-first, flag-second — borderline cases like paper’s .89/.93 alcohol component stay visible); report endpoint r (direct, -levels edges) alongside chain flag disagree (correlation non-transitive: clean adjacent chain neither implies implied endpoint identity — chain answers “perpetuates every level,” endpoint r answers “construct”). 21. Artefact → never auto-flagged. prune = "artefact" surfaces φ inspection; removal documented researcher judgment (Forbes explicit introduces researcher DoF / confirmation bias; cf. Wicherts et al. 2016). Known limitations / deferred future milestones: - factor_cor ESEM engine permuted variance-sort ord vector. Safe permanently: orthogonal rotation supported (factor_cor = ; permutation ), cfQ/oblique scope (§9, §14.1). guard comment engine_esem.R documents required decision ever reversed. - Algebra-vs-scores cross-check cover cor = "polychoric" paths (see ). - cor = "spearman" + method = "esem" semantically inconsistent (lavaan fits Pearson ML raw data edges use Spearman R); currently accepted without warning. - ESEM engine detect warn improper/Heywood solutions (lavaan negative residual variances), unlike EFA engine warns explicitly. - Forbes-extension improvements deferred past M5 (published method weaker spots worth strengthening later; M5 ships faithful method + §14.20 reporting enrichments): - Structural artefact signals. Beyond congruence, artefacts detectable structural signatures — split--merge pattern (indicators split level k reunite different parent k+1; Forbes Fig. 2), factors < 3 primary-loading items, orphaned/non-replicating factors. Surface diagnostics rather relying φ alone. - Factor-score-indeterminacy caveat EFA/ESEM redundancy. Score-correlation-based redundancy exact PCA (Waller algebra) inherits factor-score indeterminacy EFA/ESEM; φ (loading-based) stable criterion . Consider making φ default non-PCA engines. - Selection bias “strongest” edge. Plotting max correlation across many -levels pairs capitalizes chance (85 → 1,320 correlations levels grow). Add bootstrap CIs / SEs edges (reuse loadings_se infrastructure) strongest-edge claim inferentially honest.

title: Choosing an Engine: PCA, EFA, and ESEM
text: PCA extracts principal components — linear combinations observed variables capture maximum variance, including measurement error. Every item modeled communality 1.0: components account 100% item’s variance. true latent variable model; data reduction method. bass-ackwards context, PCA natural default. fast, always converges, produces eigenvalues can guide choice k. Waller (2007) showed -level algebra (W'RW) holds exactly components, making edges algebraically exact rather approximated materialized scores. “fit” PCA just eigenvalue component — amount variance captures. chi-square tests, RMSEA, model rejection.
code: x_pca <- ackwards(bfi, k = 3, cor = "polychoric") x_pca #>  #> ── Bass-Ackwards Analysis (ackwards) ─────────────────────────────────────────── #> Engine: pca #> Rotation: cfT #> Basis: polychoric #> n: 2,436 #> k (max): 3 #>  #> ── Levels ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: 1 factor, 22.9% variance #> ✔ k = 2: 2 factors, 34.7% variance #> ✔ k = 3: 3 factors, 43.9% variance #>  #> ── Edges ── #>  #> 5 of 8 edges have |r| ≥ 0.3 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: This is a series of linked solutions, not a fitted hierarchical model. #> Cross-level edges are descriptive score correlations. tidy(x_pca, what = "fit") #>   level           index    value #> 1     1 eigenvalue.m1f1 5.725295 #> 2     2 eigenvalue.m2f1 5.725295 #> 3     2 eigenvalue.m2f2 2.960038 #> 4     3 eigenvalue.m3f1 5.725295 #> 5     3 eigenvalue.m3f2 2.960038 #> 6     3 eigenvalue.m3f3 2.293698

title: Choosing an Engine: PCA, EFA, and ESEM
text: EFA extracts latent factors model variance shared among items. item retains unique variance (communality < 1.0) factors explain. classical common-factor model, appropriate PCA believe items fallible indicators latent constructs rather constructs . EFA produces genuine goodness--fit indices. tell whether k factors sufficient reproduce observed correlation matrix within sampling error. RMSEA values large (> 0.10), indicating 1–3 factors fully account BFI item correlations — unsurprising, true structure 5 factors. Fit improves steadily k = 1 k = 3, exactly kind evidence bass-ackwards analysis designed make visible.

what: ESEM: EFA with full model diagnostics
title: Choosing an Engine: PCA, EFA, and ESEM
text: ESEM (exploratory structural equation modeling, Asparouhov & Muthén, 2009) fits common-factor model EFA uses lavaan engine. unlocks two capabilities EFA provide: Standard errors every loading, enabling confidence intervals significance tests. WLSMV estimator ordinal data, appropriate maximum-likelihood-adjacent estimator categorical indicators. cor = "polychoric" set method = "esem", WLSMV used automatically. ESEM fit indices include CFI SRMR addition RMSEA TLI, giving richer picture model adequacy.

what: prune = "artefact": factors defined by structural similarity
title: The Forbes Extension: Skip-Level Connections and Pruning
text: artefact factor one whose loading pattern similar factor non-adjacent level -level neighbors. Similarity measured Tucker’s congruence coefficient (φ): ϕ(Fa,Fb)=∑iλiaλib∑iλia2⋅∑iλib2\phi(F_a, F_b) = \frac{\sum_i \lambda_{ia}\lambda_{ib}} {\sqrt{\sum_i \lambda_{ia}^2 \cdot \sum_i \lambda_{ib}^2}} φ ranges −1 +1, values > 0.95 indicating near-identical loading patterns regardless sign. factor k = 3 φ > 0.95 factor k = 1 suspected artefact rotation rather genuine new dimension. BFI, artefact criterion flags nothing — factor level loading pattern similar factor non-adjacent level -level solution. good result well-validated instrument: suggests rotation producing genuinely distinct factors level, recycling old ones. data weaker noisier factor structure, rotations struggle separate highly correlated factors, artefact criterion often flag nodes. node can flagged one criterion ; two criteria capture different flavors redundancy: "redundant": factor appears multiple levels near-identical score correlations — persists unchanged k increases. "artefact": factor’s loading pattern closely resembles factor different, non-adjacent level — looks like copy rather refinement.
code: x_art <- ackwards(bfi,   k = 5, cor = "polychoric",   pairs = "all", prune = "artefact" ) #> ℹ Artefact mode: Tucker's computed for all cross-level factor pairs. #> ℹ Inspect `x$prune$phi` to identify potential artefacts; removal is a #>   researcher judgment (Forbes, 2023). tidy(x_art, what = "nodes") #>      id level pruned prune_reason #> 1  m1f1     1  FALSE         <NA> #> 2  m2f1     2  FALSE         <NA> #> 3  m2f2     2  FALSE         <NA> #> 4  m3f1     3  FALSE         <NA> #> 5  m3f2     3  FALSE         <NA> #> 6  m3f3     3  FALSE         <NA> #> 7  m4f1     4  FALSE         <NA> #> 8  m4f2     4  FALSE         <NA> #> 9  m4f3     4  FALSE         <NA> #> 10 m4f4     4  FALSE         <NA> #> 11 m5f1     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 12 m5f2     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 13 m5f3     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 14 m5f4     5  FALSE         <NA> #> 15 m5f5     5  FALSE         <NA>

BFI, result across wide range thresholds redundant chains correlations > 0.97 — flagging unambiguous. data may find borderline cases threshold matters: BFI thresholds agree: flagged factors robustly redundant, borderline cases. noisier datasets smaller samples typically see count increase lower threshold.
code: # Check how many factors are flagged at different redundancy_r thresholds thresholds <- c(0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95) counts <- sapply(thresholds, function(thr) {   x <- suppressWarnings(     ackwards(bfi,       k = 5, cor = "polychoric",       pairs = "all", prune = "redundant",       redundancy_r = thr     )   )   sum(tidy(x, what = "nodes")$pruned) }) #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.8) flagged 8 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.85) flagged 7 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.9) flagged 6 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. #> ℹ Redundancy pruning (|r| ≥ 0.95) flagged 6 nodes. #> ℹ Nodes are retained in the object; inspect with `x$prune$nodes` and #>   `x$prune$chains`. data.frame(redundancy_r = thresholds, n_flagged = counts) #>   redundancy_r n_flagged #> 1         0.80         8 #> 2         0.85         7 #> 3         0.90         6 #> 4         0.95         6

title: The Forbes Extension: Skip-Level Connections and Pruning

(pairs = "") Skip-level correlations near 1.0 indicate stable dimensions survive changes k — exactly kind robust construct want report. levels factor structure just reorganizing rather genuinely differentiating? (prune = "redundant") Flagged levels can often removed k range without losing interpretive content. common workflow: fit pairs = "" first examine full picture, apply prune = "redundant" identify levels add new information, use guide focus reporting.

what: The problem: which factor solution is right?
title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: Anyone applied factor analysis faces dilemma: many factors extract? Parallel analysis might say 5; scree plot might suggest 3; reviewer might insist 1. solution seems describe data differently, tempting treat competing answers question. Goldberg (2006) reframed problem: different solutions competing — complementary. 1-factor solution captures broadest shared variance data, dimension along variables correlate. 5-factor solution captures narrower, specific dimensions. two levels resolution describe underlying structure different vantage points, just satellite image street map describe city. bass-ackwards method makes relationship explicit. fits factor models every level 1 k, computes correlations factor scores across adjacent levels. correlations show : narrow factor inherits broad factor — lineage dimension move coarse fine resolution. factor splits — single broad factor correlates strongly two narrower factors fragmenting sub-dimensions. factor stable — factor correlates ~1.0 counterpart adjacent level essentially construct appearing two resolutions. result hierarchy map — visual numerical account factor structure data builds broad narrow. Note: descriptive, data-driven hierarchy, confirmatory hierarchical model like Schmid–Leiman higher-order SEM. -level correlations score correlations (algebraic equivalents), model parameters. See vignette("engines") use EFA ESEM instead PCA.

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: use 25-item Big Five Inventory psych package (psych::bfi). items measure five personality traits — Agreeableness (A1–A5), Conscientiousness (C1–C5), Extraversion (E1–E5), Neuroticism (N1–N5), Openness (O1–O5) — 6-point Likert scale, 2,800 participants. drop 364 cases missing item, leaving n = 2,436. real analysis might use pairwise deletion multiple imputation; na.omit() sufficient illustration.
code: library(ackwards)  bfi <- na.omit(psych::bfi[, 1:25]) dim(bfi) #> [1] 2436   25

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: fitting hierarchy, helps sense plausible range k. suggest_k() runs two established selection criteria: Parallel analysis (Horn, 1965): compares observed eigenvalues random data size suggests retaining factors whose observed eigenvalues exceed parallel-analysis threshold. Velicer’s MAP criterion (Velicer, 1976): examines average -diagonal partial correlation successive factors extracted; suggests k average minimized. warning? suggest_k() detected BFI items look ordinal (integer-valued, ≤ 7 distinct values). default still uses Pearson correlations, can underestimate factor loadings ordinal items. move ackwards(), can address cor = "polychoric" — next. criteria agree: k = 5. matches instrument design (Big Five) gives us clear target. note bottom reminds us k ackwards() upper bound — hierarchy covers every level 1 k, setting k one two levels higher consensus often informative.
code: sk <- suggest_k(bfi) #> ℹ Running parallel analysis (20 iterations)... #> ✔ Running parallel analysis (20 iterations)... [118ms] #>  #> ℹ Running MAP (Velicer)... #> ✔ Running MAP (Velicer)... [123ms] #>  sk #>  #> ── Factor / Component Count Suggestion (ackwards) ────────────────────────────── #> Variables: 25 #> n: 2,436 #> Basis: pearson #> Tested k: 1–8 #>  #> ── Criteria (k = 1–8) ── #>  #> ✔ k = 1: MAP = 0.0249 | PA suggested #> ✔ k = 2: MAP = 0.0189 | PA suggested #> ✔ k = 3: MAP = 0.0175 | PA suggested #> ✔ k = 4: MAP = 0.0157 | PA suggested #> ✔ k = 5: MAP = 0.0146 | PA suggested #> - k = 6: MAP = 0.0160 | PA not suggested #> - k = 7: MAP = 0.0194 | PA not suggested #> - k = 8: MAP = 0.0222 | PA not suggested #>  #> ── Recommendations ── #>  #> • Parallel analysis: k ≤ 5 #> • MAP (Velicer): k = 5 #> Consensus: k = 5 #> ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── #> Note: k in ackwards() is a maximum depth. Consider setting k one or two levels #> above the consensus to observe factor fragmentation. #> Caution: parallel analysis tends to overextract; many suggested structures do #> not replicate (Forbes, 2023). Treat this as a range.

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: print() gives quick overview: “Levels” section confirms five models converged, reports cumulative variance explained level. Notice jump k = 1 k = 2 large (22.9% → 34.7%), later jumps smaller — characteristic data strong general factor several specific dimensions. “Edges” section reports many 40 possible -level connections exceed display threshold |r| ≥ 0.3. 40 comes summing across adjacent pairs: 1×2 + 2×3 + 3×4 + 4×5 = 2 + 6 + 12 + 20 = 40. glance() returns information one-row data frame, convenient comparisons across models:

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: hierarchy diagram centerpiece method. column represents one level (k = 1 left, k = 5 right). Arrows connect factor primary parent — factor level strongest correlation (|r|). Arrow thickness proportional |r|; color shows direction (blue = positive, orange = negative default); solid arrows indicate strong connections (|r| ≥ 0.6), dashed arrows show moderate ones (|r| ≥ 0.3).  Reading diagram left right tells story Big Five: k = 1: One broad factor. Given polychoric basis, captures shared variance across 25 items. k = 2: broad factor splits two: one become Extraversion/Agreeableness/Conscientiousness one become Neuroticism/Openness. k = 3: Three factors emerge, Neuroticism separating . k = 4: Agreeableness Extraversion begin differentiate. k = 5: canonical Big Five appear. factors labeled m{k}f{j} (level k, factor j). stable IDs used throughout object, m5f1 k = 5 refers factor loadings, edge table, factor scores, diagram.

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: Factor scores place every observation factor every level. useful regression, clustering, downstream analysis want continuous summary latent dimension. augment(x, data = bfi) computes scores fly stored weight matrices appends data frame. score columns named .m{k}f{j} distinguish original variables. Scores standardized model-implied variance 1 — meaning scaling comes polychoric correlation matrix, raw data. practice empirical standard deviations close exactly 1 non-Pearson basis used; see ?augment.ackwards details. pure PCA Pearson correlations model-implied empirical variances agree exactly. need scores repeatedly (e.g., cross-validation loop), store fit time scores = TRUE avoid re-applying weight matrices every call:
code: scored <- augment(x, data = bfi) #> Warning: ! Factor scores are standardized using model-implied SDs from a "polychoric" #>   correlation matrix. #> ℹ The raw projection uses `scale(data)` (Pearson z-scores), but `score_var` #>   comes from the "polychoric" R. #> ℹ Empirical score SDs will differ from 1.0. For non-Pearson analyses, #>   between-level edges from `tidy()` are the authoritative associations. #> This warning is displayed once per session. dim(scored) # 25 original items + 15 score columns (1+2+3+4+5) #> [1] 2436   40 names(scored)[26:40] #>  [1] ".m1f1" ".m2f1" ".m2f2" ".m3f1" ".m3f2" ".m3f3" ".m4f1" ".m4f2" ".m4f3" #> [10] ".m4f4" ".m5f1" ".m5f2" ".m5f3" ".m5f4" ".m5f5" x2 <- ackwards(bfi, k = 5, cor = "polychoric", scores = TRUE) scored2 <- augment(x2) # uses stored matrices — no recomputation identical(round(scored2$.m5f1, 8), round(scored$.m5f1, 8)) #> [1] TRUE

title: Introduction to Bass-Ackwards Analysis
text: bass-ackwards workflow ackwards six steps: suggest_k(data) — identify plausible range hierarchy depth. ackwards(data, k, cor = ...) — fit full hierarchy factor models. print(x) / glance(x) — check levels converged read top-level summary. autoplot(x) — visualize hierarchy lineage diagram. tidy(x, = ...) — extract loadings, edges, variance form ready tables analysis. augment(x, data = ...) — generate factor scores downstream use.

what: Why Pearson underestimates for ordinal items
title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: continuous latent trait sliced small number ordered categories, observed correlation two items always lower correlation underlying latent variables. coarse rating scale, larger attenuation. 5-point scale, Pearson correlations can 10–20% lower true latent correlations; 2-point (binary) scale, underestimate can exceed 30%. matters bass-ackwards analysis : Loadings attenuated. Factors appear weaker . -level edges attenuated. hierarchy looks flatter — factors nearly perfectly correlated across levels may appear moderately correlated. number factors underestimated. Attenuation compresses eigenvalue spectrum, selection criteria (parallel analysis, MAP) may suggest fewer factors truly exist.

what: Practical guidance: when to use polychoric
title: Ordinal Data: Polychoric Correlations and WLSMV
text: Polychoric estimation requires fitting bivariate normal model every item pair. p items p(p−1)/2 pairs — 300 25-item scale. Computation usually fast (seconds), scales O(p²) can slow large item pools. note score computation. cor = "polychoric", weight matrices stored object derived polychoric R. Materializing factor scores via augment() applies weights Pearson-standardized raw data (scale(data)). resulting scores calibrated model-implied unit variance, empirically unit variance. downstream analysis distinction rarely matters — scores still well-scaled — augment() issues warning cor != "pearson". -level edges tidy(= "edges") always exact come algebra, materialized scores.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis R ackwards implements Goldberg’s (2006) bass-ackwards method modern extensions mapping hierarchical structure multivariate data. core insight simple: instead asking many factors best describe data, ask solutions different levels resolution related one another. 1-factor solution captures broadest shared variance; 5-factor solution captures narrower, specific dimensions. Bass-ackwards analysis traces broad factors split narrow ones — narrow factors redundant re-combinations broader ones. package supports three extraction engines (PCA, EFA, ESEM), polychoric correlations ordinal data, Forbes’s (2023) extension reveals skip-level connections flags redundant artefactual factors.

title: Bass-Ackwards Hierarchical Structural Analysis
text: autoplot() draws hierarchical diagram. column level (k = 1 left, k = 5 right); arrows show narrow factors inherit broad factor. Solid arrows indicate strong connections (|r| ≥ 0.6 default); dashed arrows show weaker ones.  five-factor level cleanly splits Big Five. single broad factor k = 1 (roughly general positive character) differentiates first positive vs. negative affect (k = 2), successively narrower traits.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: data data frame numeric matrix observed variables (items columns, observations rows). Missing values handled via pairwise deletion computing R. Note: n_obs passed EFA engine always nrow(data); missingness effective per-correlation N may smaller, making chi-square / RMSEA / p-value slightly anti-conservative. k Maximum number factors/components. Required; use suggest_k() uncertain. Sets depth hierarchy: levels 1 k extracted. method Extraction engine: "pca" (default), "efa", "esem". "esem" uses lavaan::efa() rotation-aware SEs per-level fit indices; recommended clinical/HiTOP workflow (Kim & Eaton, 2015; Forbush et al., 2024). Requires lavaan >= 0.6-13. rotation Rotation family. "cfT" (orthogonal Crawford-Ferguson, default, ≈ varimax) supported. Oblique rotation ("cfQ") available: oblique within-level factor correlations confound -level score correlations method's core output (Goldberg, 2006; Kim & Eaton, 2015). See DESIGN.md §9. kappa CF rotation kappa parameter. NULL (default) uses 1 / p p number variables — value reproduces varimax orthogonal rotation (Crawford & Ferguson, 1970; Browne, 2001; Kim & Eaton, 2015). cor Correlation basis: "pearson" (default), "spearman", "polychoric". PCA/EFA, "polychoric" computes polychoric correlation matrix via psych::polychoric() (requires psych). ESEM, triggers WLSMV estimation via lavaan. cli warning emitted ordinal-looking columns detected cor "polychoric". fm Factor extraction method passed psych::fa(); used method = "efa". One "minres" (default, robust OLS), "ml" (maximum likelihood, gives chi-square fit converges less reliably deep levels), "pa" (principal axis). Ignored method = "pca". estimator Estimation method ESEM engine. NULL (default) auto-selects: "WLSMV" cor = "polychoric", "ML" otherwise. Pass explicitly override: "ULSMV" (unweighted WLS), "MLR" (robust ML). Ignored PCA EFA engines. align Logical; sign-align factors primary-parent lineage? Default TRUE. scores Logical; store factor scores result? Default FALSE (recomputable via augment.ackwards()). TRUE, per-observation scores stored x$scores named list n × k_j matrices, one per level, standardized real score SDs (see augment.ackwards()). keep_fits Logical; store raw engine fit objects? Default FALSE. TRUE, per-level fit objects (psych lavaan) stored x$fits named list indexed level. seed Integer seed stochastic engines (used PCA captured reproducibility metadata). Default NULL. pairs level pairs compute edges : "adjacent" (default, classic Goldberg — consecutive levels) "" (Forbes extension — every pair levels). "" reveals associations span multiple levels required redundancy pruning. Setting prune anything "none" automatically upgrades "" message. prune Character vector controlling Forbes-extension pruning. Default "none" (pruning). Options: "redundant" — identify chains factors connected primary-parent links |r| >= redundancy_r (optionally phi > redundancy_phi). Applies Forbes's (2023) retention rule: keep bottom node chain reaches level k (specific); keep top node otherwise. Pruning flag-: flagged nodes stay object pruned = TRUE prune_reason = "redundant" x$prune$nodes. "artefact" — compute Tucker's congruence coefficient (φ) cross-level factor pairs store x$prune$phi researcher inspection. factors auto-flagged; artefact identification requires judgment (Forbes, 2023; Wicherts et al., 2016). redundancy_r Scalar (0, 1]. Adjacent primary-parent |r| threshold redundancy chains. Default 0.9 (Forbes, 2023). redundancy_phi Scalar (0, 1] NULL (default). non-NULL, Tucker's φ must also exceed threshold link included redundancy chain (conjunctive redundancy_r). NULL means redundancy_r used. Recommended: 0.95 (Lorenzo-Seva & ten Berge, 2006). cut_show Edges |r| >= cut_show flagged above_cut tidy() output. Default 0.3. ... Reserved future arguments.

title: Bass-ackwards hierarchical structural analysis — ackwards
text: method = "pca" — original Goldberg (2006) method; fastest; never fails converge; Waller (2007) algebra exact components. rotation = "cfT" — orthogonal Crawford-Ferguson (≈ varimax) keeps within-level factors uncorrelated, cross-level edges reflect hierarchical signal, within-level factor covariance. Matches Goldberg. cor = "pearson" — silent basis switching. items look ordinal (≤ 7 distinct integer values), cli warning suggest cor = "polychoric", available three engines. align = TRUE — unaligned signs make output unreadable. Anchor: m1f1 oriented toward positive manifold; subsequent factor flipped edge primary parent positive. scores = FALSE / keep_fits = FALSE — memory privacy. Scores O(n × Σk) often sensitive; raw engine fits can large. recomputable stored r matrix.

text: Score computation. Scores S = Z W / sqrt(score_var), Z = scale(data) (item z-scores), W per-level weight matrix stored object, sqrt(score_var) standardizes real score standard deviations (Invariant 1: never assume unit variance). PCA method "components"; EFA/ESEM "tenBerge". Missing data. Score projection applies weights row-wise propagates NAs listwise: observation least one missing item variable produce NA scores every level. differs fitting, uses pairwise-complete correlations. warning issued NA rows detected. Use na.omit(data) scoring NA rows unwanted. Data source. data supplied, scores always recomputed using stored weights — score new observations. data NULL scores = TRUE set fit time, stored scores returned. neither available informative error raised.

title: Suggest a maximum number of factors for bass-ackwards analysis — suggest_k
text: Criteria computed: Parallel analysis (Horn 1965) – compares observed eigenvalues random correlation matrices size. Suggests number components whose eigenvalues exceed 95th-percentile chance level. MAP (Velicer 1976) – Minimum Average Partial criterion.
